title: ProtocolLab Core Contract Coverage Model
purpose: Describe how the public repository organizes core contract surfaces across SpecTrace requirements, schemas, fixtures, documentation, and traceability links.
design_summary: The public repository separates contract selection, capability discovery, declarative scenario and suite data, load intent, run-plan binding, evidence contracts, and fixture examples. JSON Schemas define machine-readable shapes. SpecTrace JSON artifacts define normative requirements and verification. Markdown supports discovery and explanation only.
data_and_state_considerations: All core contract artifacts are static repository documents. Runtime state, worker placement, package materialization, launch mechanics, telemetry collection, and hosted lab behavior remain implementation-owned.
edge_cases_and_constraints: A structurally valid run plan can still be incompatible when selected packages cannot satisfy requested scenarios or capabilities.
A valid suite selects scenarios but does not make performance claims by itself.
A valid load profile constrains load intent and evidence tier, but it does not define how traffic is generated.
Adapter and test-executor telemetry discovery is optional and cannot change conformance pass/fail status by itself.
Invalid fixtures should each demonstrate one clear contract failure.
alternatives_considered: Shipping executable validators was rejected because public validation automation would reintroduce runner behavior.
Treating Markdown pages as canonical requirements was rejected because SpecTrace JSON is the repository-native requirements standard.
Defining generated SDKs as the contract source of truth was rejected because the contracts must remain language-neutral.
risks: YAML examples need schema paths and fixtures to stay discoverable because this repository does not execute validation scripts.
Convenience traceability indexes can drift unless canonical SpecTrace artifacts remain the authority.
Optional telemetry examples can be mistaken for required backends unless non-dependency language remains explicit.
